title: Vector Spaces and Gram-Schmidt Process
summary: Deep dive into vector spaces, subspaces, span, linear independence, basis, and the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization.
content_html:
Span

The span of vectors {v₁, v₂, ..., vₙ} is the set of all linear combinations:

span{v₁,...,vₙ} = {c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₙvₙ : cᵢ ∈ ℝ}

Subspace

A subset W of vector space V is a subspace if:

  • 0 ∈ W (contains zero vector)
  • Closed under addition: u, v ∈ W ⟹ u + v ∈ W
  • Closed under scalar multiplication: v ∈ W, k ∈ ℝ ⟹ kv ∈ W

Testing Linear Independence

Set up c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₙvₙ = 0 as a matrix equation, then check if the only solution is c = 0.

v₁=(1,2,3), v₂=(0,1,2), v₃=(1,1,1)
Form matrix A = [v₁|v₂|v₃] and row-reduce.
If rank(A) = 3 → independent.  If rank < 3 → dependent.

Orthogonal and Orthonormal Sets

  • Orthogonal set: every pair of vectors satisfies vᵢ · vⱼ = 0 for i ≠ j
  • Orthonormal set: orthogonal AND every vector has magnitude 1 (|vᵢ| = 1)

Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization — Full Algorithm

Given linearly independent {v₁, v₂, v₃}:

Step 1: u₁ = v₁

Step 2: u₂ = v₂ - proj_{u₁}(v₂) = v₂ - (v₂·u₁/|u₁|²)u₁

Step 3: u₃ = v₃ - proj_{u₁}(v₃) - proj_{u₂}(v₃)
           = v₃ - (v₃·u₁/|u₁|²)u₁ - (v₃·u₂/|u₂|²)u₂

Normalize: e₁ = u₁/|u₁|,  e₂ = u₂/|u₂|,  e₃ = u₃/|u₃|

Worked Example

v₁ = (1,1,0),  v₂ = (1,0,1),  v₃ = (0,1,1)

u₁ = (1,1,0),  |u₁|² = 2

v₂·u₁ = 1·1+0·1+1·0 = 1
u₂ = (1,0,1) - (1/2)(1,1,0) = (1/2, -1/2, 1)
|u₂|² = 1/4+1/4+1 = 3/2

v₃·u₁ = 1, v₃·u₂ = 0·(1/2)+1·(-1/2)+1·1 = 1/2
u₃ = (0,1,1)-(1/2)(1,1,0)-(1/3)(1/2,-1/2,1)
   = (0,1,1)-(1/2,1/2,0)-(1/6,-1/6,1/3)
   = (-2/3, 2/3, 2/3)

Normalize each uᵢ to get orthonormal basis {e₁,e₂,e₃}.

Why Orthonormal Bases are Useful

With an orthonormal basis {e₁,...,eₙ}, any vector v can be expressed as v = (v·e₁)e₁ + (v·e₂)e₂ + ... without solving a linear system.
